paragraphs.text: Since the Giver, God, had appointed that in the next year the gift would be sent down to Abraham, Abraham was considering whether or not the time would be revealed to him at which he should receive the blessing and Sarah’s closed womb should be opened. While Abraham was thinking on this, “the Lord… appeared to him… as he sat at the door of his tent in the heat of the day” (Gen 18:1). Abraham wished to satisfy the eyes of his soul with this revelation, but God became invisible to him. While he was still wondering why God had appeared to him and then, without saying anything, had hidden Himself, “he saw, and behold, three men stood beside him”; and then, laying aside his thought, “he ran to meet them from the door of the tent” (Gen 18:2).

When Abraham hastened to them from his tent, as toward strangers, and by this haste manifested his love of hospitality, then God appears to him, and at the door of the tent clearly shows Himself in one of the three strangers. And Abraham “bowed down to the earth” (Gen 18:2), entreating Him in whom the divine greatness was revealed to enter his house and bless his dwelling: “If… I have found favor before Thee, pass not by Thy servant” (Gen 18:3). The One entreated by Abraham did not refuse, and answered: “So do, as thou hast said.” Then “Abraham hastened… to Sarah and said to her… Knead three measures of fine meal” (Gen 18:5–6); while he himself hastened to the herds to bring a fat calf. Abraham offered bread and meat in such abundance not only to satisfy the angels, but to share the blessing with all his household.

When the strangers’ feet had been washed and they sat down under the tree, Abraham brought and set before them all that was prepared; but he himself did not dare to sit, but stood before them, as is fitting for a servant. Having tasted of the meal offered by Abraham, they ask about Sarah; and Sarah, who even in old age preserved modesty, comes out of the inner chamber to the door of the tent. For by Abraham’s solicitude, and by the silence which all in the house kept at his sign, the household understood that these were not simple strangers stretching their feet out to be washed by a man of God. Then the Lord says to Abraham and Sarah: “I will certainly return to thee according to the time of life; and, lo, Sarah shall have a son” (Gen 18:10). But Sarah, although Abraham, standing behind, strengthened her in hope, “laughed within herself, saying: After I have waxed old shall I be young again? and my lord is old also” (Gen 18:12).

If Sarah had asked for a sign, a sign would have been given her — first, because she was barren and old; and second, because nothing yet had happened like what was promised her, by seeing or hearing of which she might have believed. But although she did not ask for a sign, a sign was given to her in herself and in her own person. The Lord said: “Why did Sarah laugh within herself, saying: Shall I of a surety bear a child? for I am old” (Gen 18:13). But Sarah, instead of accepting the true sign given to her, covers it with a lie: “Sarah denied, saying: I laughed not” (Gen 18:15). And the angel, giving her to understand that her excuse by a lie was in vain, says: “Nay; but thou didst laugh (Gen 18:15) in thy heart; behold, thy heart refutes the empty word of thy tongue.”

The angels who pronounced the promise to Sarah of the birth of a son “arose… and looked toward Sodom” (Gen 18:16). But Sarah was not told that they were going to Sodom, lest on the day she was gladdened by the promise of a son, she should be grieved for her brother by the announcement of the dread sentence of judgment pronounced upon the Sodomites and their kindred. Therefore they hid this from Sarah, so that she should not shed tears continually. But to Abraham it was revealed, that he should not cease to pray, and that his prayer might reveal to the world that in Sodom not even one righteous man was found for whose sake it could be delivered from destruction. “The cry of Sodom and Gomorrah is multiplied unto Me, and their sins are very grievous” (Gen 18:20). What is meant by the word “cry” is explained by what is said following about their sins. “I will go down now, and see whether they have done altogether according to the cry of it, which is come unto Me; and if not, that I may know” (Gen 18:21). The Lord said this not because He did not know their sins. Earlier He had already said: “their sins are very grievous”; but He wished by this to give judges an example, that they should not pronounce sentence before seeing the case fully. If the All-Knowing One presented Himself as if He did not know, in order not to determine punishment before judgment, how much more should judges acknowledge their ignorance and not pronounce sentence before investigation of the case.
